column2: Set interface name without msg in ROS2  When creating custom message types in ROS1 (noetic), I can store the *.msg files in a subfolder named msg. In the CMake file, I just type the name of the file without msg/ in add_message_files(...). The resulting message type is named as <pkg_name>/<file-name (without .msg)>  When creating interfaces in ROS2 (foxy), I have to type the file name in my CMake file including the relative path in rosidl_generate_interfaces(...) like msg/<filename.msg>. The resulting interface name also includes msg: <pkg_name>/msg/<file-name (without .msg)>, but actually I don't want the exact path as an interface name.  So my question is: Is it possible to store the files in the msg folder for a better folder structure but set the name of the resulting interface differently? In ROS2 (e.g., Foxy), custom interface files (.msg, .srv, .action) are typically stored in subfolders like msg/ for clarity. When you reference them in CMake using `rosidl_generate_interfaces()`, you provide the relative path (e.g., `msg/MyCustom.msg`). Unlike ROS1, ROS2 automatically namespaces the interface as `<pkg_name>/msg/MyCustom`, including the folder name in the interface type. Currently, ROS2 does not offer a built-in way to change or omit the `msg/` part from the message type while retaining the folder structure; the interface type will always In ROS2 (such as Foxy), interface files like messages are conventionally stored in a msg/ directory for organizational clarity. When you specify them in rosidl_generate_interfaces() in CMake, you typically provide the path as msg/Filename.msg, and by default, the resulting interface type is named <package_name>/msg/Filename. Unlike ROS1, ROS2 uses the folder structure as part of the fully qualified interface name for clarity and to avoid naming conflicts. Currently, ROS2 does not support customizing or omitting the folder prefix (e.g., removing ‘msg/’ from the interface type name) while still using that In ROS2 (Foxy and later), interface definition files such as messages (*.msg), services (*.srv), and actions (*.action) are conventionally placed in subfolders like msg/, srv/, or action/ within your package. When using rosidl_generate_interfaces() in CMake, you usually specify the path (e.g., msg/MyMsg.msg). As a result, the generated interface types are named with the subfolder included, such as <package_name>::msg::MyMsg for C++ or <package_name>/msg/MyMsg for Python. Currently, ROS2 enforces this naming scheme and does not natively support aliasing In ROS2 (e.g., Foxy), interface files such as messages (*.msg) are conventionally stored in a subdirectory named "msg" for organization, similar to ROS1. However, unlike ROS1, the resulting interface type in ROS2 includes the folder path—such as <pkg_name>/msg/<MessageType>—because ROS2 uses the full relative path specified in the `rosidl_generate_interfaces()` macro in CMake. At present, there is no supported mechanism to rename or alias message types to remove the "msg/" component from their fully qualified names when using the standard build tools; the interface name will always In ROS2 (e.g., Foxy), when defining custom message types, interface files are typically placed in a `msg/` directory for organization. By default, the interface name will include this folder as a namespace (e.g., `<package>/msg/<Message>`). This behavior differs from ROS1, where message types do not inherit the subfolder in their type name. Currently in ROS2, the `rosidl_generate_interfaces()` macro requires specifying the relative path (like `msg/Foo.msg`). There is no official way to exclude the `msg/` namespace from the generated interface name while keeping the `.msg` files

I have looked into A* and that could work when using cubic or quintic splines as that could account for the current velocity, but when accounting for more sophisticated object avoidance using something like rrt, I am unsure as to how that would work with a non zero starting velocity. Thank you! Path and trajectory planning for holonomic drivetrains—such as those found in omnidirectional robots—requires special consideration when the robot begins with non-zero velocities.
